steps.title: Match the needed support
steps.content: The student claims Aristotle says obedience to law should be nearly absolute and that even minor infractions should not be ignored. The best quotation must connect small violations of law to serious damage to the state.

steps.title: Reject different political issues
steps.content: Choice B concerns preventing magistrates from making money and focuses on corruption, especially bribery in oligarchies. Choice C concerns preserving constitutions by keeping their destroyers near or far enough to create caution. Choice D concerns why oligarchies may last because rulers maintain good terms with different groups.

steps.title: Focus on small violations
steps.content: The relevant quotation says transgression creeps in unnoticed and eventually ruins the state. Its comparison to small expenditures slowly depleting a fortune supports the idea that minor infractions should not be dismissed. A quotation about corruption, external threats, or relations among classes might concern political stability, but it would not show why small violations of law should be treated seriously.

steps.title: Keep the support precise
steps.content: The quotation about transgression is also broad enough to support the claim about a healthy state generally. It is not limited to one form of government or one temporary political situation, so it fits the student’s general claim about preserving order through strict obedience to law.

steps.title: Connect quote to claim
steps.content: That evidence directly supports the student’s claim about strict obedience to law because it shows Aristotle warning that even seemingly small violations can accumulate into major political instability. The answer is A.
